steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction, biliary colic without cholecystitis, GERD/peptic ulcer disease
ensemble.information_gap: Whether gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or biliary ductal dilation are present — i.e., whether the biliary tract is the anatomic source of this patient's RUQ/epigastric pain
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallstones with or without sonographic Murphy's sign, gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, or common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has a documented history of biliary colic with multiple ED visits, RUQ/epigastric tenderness on exam, mildly elevated AST and ALT, a borderline elevated lipase, and leukocytosis with neutrophilia — all pointing toward a biliary etiology that ultrasound is the first-line, radiation-free modality to evaluate.
grounding: History: 'history of biliary colic multiple ED presentations, now w/RUQ pain beginning ~12 hrs prior'
Physical examination: 'RUQ, epigastric TTP, no palpable masses'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient's known history of biliary colic, RUQ/epigastric tenderness, mildly elevated transaminases, borderline lipase, and leukocytosis with neutrophilia all raise concern for an acute biliary process — most likely acute cholecystitis or choledocholithiasis, with gallstone pancreatitis also possible given the lipase just above the upper limit of normal. Ultrasound of the right upper quadrant is the appropriate first imaging step because it is the most sensitive and specific non-invasive modality for detecting gallstones, gallbladder inflammation, and biliary ductal dilation, without radiation exposure, and will directly address whether the biliary tract is the anatomic source of her symptoms.

Ultrasound of the right upper quadrant is the appropriate first imaging step because it is the most sensitive and specific non-invasive modality for detecting gallstones, gallbladder inflammation, and biliary ductal dilation, without radiation exposure, and will directly address whether the biliary tract is the anatomic source of her symptoms.

vindication.actual_finding: The gallbladder is filled with stones, the wall is thickened at 4 mm, and pericholecystic fluid is present, with a normal CBD of 5 mm.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report directly demonstrates gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), and pericholecystic fluid — all three of the positive criteria the physician was looking for to implicate the biliary tract as the source of pain.
